report.title: OpenAI closes $10B 'Deployment Company' PE joint venture
report.summary: OpenAI finalized DeployCo, a $10B joint venture backed by 19 PE firms including TPG, Bain, and SoftBank, raising $4B+ to embed OpenAI tools directly inside portfolio companies via forward-deployed engineers. The structure guarantees PE backers a 17.5% annual return — unusual for a software vendor — blurring the line between model provider and financial sponsor.

report.title: Anthropic launches $1.5B enterprise JV with Blackstone and Goldman
report.summary: Announced the same day as OpenAI's DeployCo, Anthropic's new firm pairs Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman, and Goldman Sachs to deploy Claude into midsize companies' core operations. Each of the three lead partners put in $300M; Apollo, General Atlantic, and Sequoia also joined.

report.title: Anthropic ships Claude Managed Agents in public beta with agent memory
report.summary: Claude Managed Agents is now in public beta — a fully managed harness for long-horizon agent work with secure sandboxing, durable sessions, and built-in tools via the managed-agents-2026-04-01 header. Agent memory also hit public beta under the same header, giving stateful agent apps a drop-in persistence layer without custom infra.

report.title: Claude Security enters public beta for Enterprise customers
report.summary: Anthropic opened Claude Security to all Claude Enterprise customers: Opus 4.7-powered code vulnerability scanning with proposed fixes, scheduled and targeted scans, and workflow integrations. Builders shipping regulated or security-sensitive code now have a first-party scan-and-fix loop without leaving the Claude platform.

report.title: OpenAI brings GPT-5.5, Codex, and Managed Agents to Amazon Bedrock
report.summary: OpenAI and AWS expanded their partnership to land GPT-5.5, Codex, and Bedrock Managed Agents in AWS environments in limited preview. Enterprises that already run workloads on Bedrock can now build with OpenAI's frontier models inside their existing IAM, compliance, and procurement workflows.

report.title: Pentagon clears 8 firms for classified AI networks, excluding Anthropic
report.summary: The DoD signed agreements with AWS, Google, Microsoft, NVIDIA, OpenAI, SpaceX, Oracle, and Reflection to deploy AI on classified networks — conspicuously excluding Anthropic, which the administration has labeled a supply chain risk over AI safety guardrails disputes. The NSA is reportedly using Anthropic's non-public Mythos model regardless.

report.title: Claude Code ships broad Week 19 update with permission and model picker fixes
report.summary: Claude Code released a wide maintenance update: smarter /model picker pulling from gateway endpoints, a new `claude project purge` command, fixed PreToolUse hook permission bypass (a security regression), Windows/PowerShell reliability fixes, and OpenTelemetry improvements. Teams running Claude Code against custom or Bedrock gateways should update.

learn.name: GPT-5.5 via Responses API
learn.oneLineDescription: OpenAI's strongest agentic coding model, now in the API with tool_search support.

detail.whatItDoes:
GPT-5.5 is OpenAI's current frontier model, available in the Responses API and Chat Completions API from April 24, 2026. It scores 88.7% on SWE-bench Verified, 82.7% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, and claims 60% fewer hallucinations than GPT-5.4. It supports a 1,000,000-token context window, full tool calling (web_search, file_search, computer_use, code_interpreter, image_generation), and the new tool_search feature that defers large tool catalogs to runtime.

Tool search is the standout new primitive for agent builders: you declare a namespace or MCP server with `defer_loading: true`, and the model fetches only the tools it actually needs for the current task. This cuts prompt token usage dramatically when your agent has dozens of possible tools. GPT-5.5 also introduces configurable `reasoning.effort` (low / medium / high / xhigh) and `text.verbosity` (low / medium) controls that let you trade compute for speed.
detail.whoItsFor: Backend engineers building multi-step coding agents, SWE automation pipelines, or customer-facing assistants that currently use GPT-5.4. Also relevant for teams with wide tool surfaces (50+ functions) who are hitting token limits or cache misses on tool definitions. The model slug is a drop-in at the API layer, but OpenAI recommends a fresh prompt baseline rather than porting legacy instructions verbatim.
pros: 88.7% SWE-bench Verified and 73.1% on Expert-SWE — leads all current publicly available models on coding benchmarks
tool_search reduces token waste on large tool surfaces and preserves prompt cache performance
1M token context window with configurable reasoning.effort and text.verbosity for latency/cost tuning
60% fewer hallucinations than GPT-5.4 per OpenAI's internal evals
cons: Prompts longer than 272K input tokens are billed at 2x input / 1.5x output for the full session — expensive for large-context retrieval workloads
Requires re-tuning prompts from scratch; carrying over GPT-5.4 system prompts degrades output quality per OpenAI's own guidance
detail.setupGuideMarkdown:
## Prerequisites
- Python 3.9+ or Node 20+
- `OPENAI_API_KEY` set in your environment (Tier 1+ account; GPT-5.5 requires a paid tier)
- `openai` SDK >= latest (supports gpt-5.5 slug)

```bash
pip install --upgrade openai
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
```

## Basic chat completion

```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5.5",
    input="Refactor this Python function to handle None inputs gracefully: ...",
    reasoning={"effort": "high"},   # low | medium | high | xhigh
    text={"verbosity": "low"},      # low | medium
)
print(response.output_text)
```

## Agentic tool use with web_search

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5.5",
    tools=[{"type": "web_search"}],
    input="What shipped in the OpenAI API this week?",
)
print(response.output_text)
```

## Tool search (deferred tool loading) — gpt-5.4+ only

Use this pattern when you have a large tool catalog and want the model to load only what it needs:

```python
crm_namespace = {
    "type": "namespace",
    "name": "crm",
    "description": "CRM tools for customer lookup and order management.",
    "tools": [
        {
            "type": "function",
            "name": "get_customer_profile",
            "description": "Fetch a customer profile by ID.",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"customer_id": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["customer_id"],
            },
        },
        {
            "type": "function",
            "name": "list_open_orders",
            "description": "List open orders for a customer ID.",
            "defer_loading": True,   # loaded via tool_search, not upfront
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"customer_id": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["customer_id"],
            },
        },
    ],
}

response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5.5",
    input="List open orders for customer CUST-12345.",
    tools=[crm_namespace, {"type": "tool_search"}],
)
print(response.output_text)
```

## Gotchas
- **Use `responses.create`, not `chat.completions.create`**, for any multi-turn or tool-calling workflow. Chat Completions works for single-turn but lacks reasoning.effort and tool_search.
- **Prompts > 272K tokens are billed at 2x input** for the entire session — watch context accumulation in long agent loops.
- **Don't port old system prompts verbatim.** GPT-5.5 interprets instructions literally; legacy over-specified prompts narrow the model's reasoning and hurt output quality.
- **Snapshot to lock behavior:** use `gpt-5.5-2026-04-23` instead of `gpt-5.5` in production to avoid unexpected behavior changes on alias updates.

learn.name: Gemini Deep Research API
learn.oneLineDescription: Trigger a full autonomous research loop — up to 160 searches, cited report — in one API call.

detail.whatItDoes:
The Gemini Deep Research API, available from April 21, 2026 via the Gemini Interactions API, exposes two autonomous research agents powered by Gemini 3.1 Pro. The standard agent (`deep-research-preview-04-2026`) targets interactive UIs with lower latency; Deep Research Max (`deep-research-max-preview-04-2026`) runs up to 160 Google searches, processes ~900K input tokens, and produces exhaustive cited reports designed for overnight batch pipelines.

Both agents support MCP server connections for private data, native chart and infographic generation in HTML, collaborative planning (you can review and edit the research plan before execution starts), and multimodal file uploads (PDFs, CSVs, images, audio, video) as grounding context. Tasks run asynchronously in the background — you fire the request, get an interaction ID, then poll until `status == 'completed'`. Max tasks can run up to 60 minutes.
detail.whoItsFor: Product teams building competitive intelligence dashboards, due-diligence automation, literature review tools, or market monitoring pipelines. Also useful for data engineers who want to replace manual analyst research loops with a scheduled API call. Requires a paid Gemini API tier — free-tier keys do not have access.
pros: 93.3% on DeepSearchQA and 54.6% on Humanity's Last Exam — top-of-leaderboard for research agents as of April 2026
MCP support lets the agent query your private databases and internal systems alongside the public web
Native chart generation means reports come back with visual elements ready for dashboards, not just prose
Collaborative planning lets you steer the research direction before the agent starts, reducing wasted compute on off-target reports
cons: Deep Research Max costs significantly more per query than standard Gemini models — up to ~160 searches and ~900K tokens per call; design cost controls into your pipeline before scaling
RPM limits are very low (2–5 RPM for Deep Research); batch pipelines must queue jobs carefully or they will hit rate limits mid-run
detail.setupGuideMarkdown:
## Prerequisites
- Python 3.9+ or Node 20+
- Gemini API key from [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com) — **paid tier required**, free tier does not include Deep Research
- `google-genai` Python SDK

```bash
pip install --upgrade google-genai
export GEMINI_API_KEY="AIza..."
```

## Fire a background research task and poll for completion

```python
import time
from google import genai

client = genai.Client()

# Use 'deep-research-preview-04-2026' for speed
# Use 'deep-research-max-preview-04-2026' for depth
interaction = client.interactions.create(
    input="Analyze the competitive landscape for AI coding assistants in 2026: key players, pricing, benchmark performance, and enterprise adoption trends.",
    agent="deep-research-max-preview-04-2026",
    background=True,   # required for Max; runs async up to 60 min
)

print(f"Research started. Interaction ID: {interaction.id}")

while True:
    interaction = client.interactions.get(interaction.id)
    if interaction.status == "completed":
        print(interaction.outputs[-1].text)
        break
    elif interaction.status == "failed":
        print(f"Failed: {interaction.error}")
        break
    print(f"Status: {interaction.status} — polling in 30s...")
    time.sleep(30)
```

## Curl equivalent (for quick testing)

```bash
# 1. Start the research task
curl -X POST "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/interactions" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "x-goog-api-key: $GEMINI_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "input": "Summarize the latest developments in solid-state battery technology.",
    "agent": "deep-research-preview-04-2026",
    "background": true
  }'

# Response contains an `id` field — use it below

# 2. Poll for results
curl -X GET "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/interactions/INTERACTION_ID" \
  -H "x-goog-api-key: $GEMINI_API_KEY"
```

## Gotchas
- **Do NOT use `generate_content` for Deep Research** — the agent is exclusively available via `client.interactions.create`. Calling it via the standard generate_content endpoint will fail.
- **`background=True` requires `store=True` implicitly** — the interaction state is persisted server-side. If you are building a multi-user app, treat interaction IDs as sensitive session tokens.
- **Paid tier only.** As of April 1, 2026, Google removed all Pro-tier models from the free tier. If your key returns a 403 on Deep Research, upgrade your billing account in Google AI Studio.
- **RPM is very low.** Design any batch pipeline with a queue and sleep between submissions. Hitting the rate limit mid-batch is painful to recover from.
- **Deep Research Max runtime** can reach 60 minutes for complex queries. Do not set aggressive timeouts in your HTTP client — use async polling instead.

learn.name: Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform (ADK)
learn.oneLineDescription: Google's unified platform replacing Vertex AI for building, governing, and running production agent fleets.

detail.whatItDoes:
Launched April 22, 2026 at Google Cloud Next '26, the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform is the successor to standalone Vertex AI. It combines Agent Studio (low-code visual builder), an upgraded graph-based Agent Development Kit (ADK) that orchestrates networks of sub-agents, Agent Runtime for long-running agents, a Memory Bank for persistent agent state, an Agent Registry with cryptographic IDs for auditing, and Agentic Defense for security. The platform surfaces directly inside the Google Cloud console — existing Vertex AI customers see the new brand without migration.

Also launched at the same event: the Data Agent Kit (preview), a portable plugin suite that installs directly into VS Code, Gemini CLI, Codex, and Claude Code, providing a Data Engineering Agent (GA), Data Science Agent (GA), and Database Observability Agent (preview). Model Garden now offers 200+ models including Gemini 3.1 Pro, Gemma 4, and third-party models like Claude Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku.
detail.whoItsFor: Platform and MLOps engineers managing production agent deployments on Google Cloud, and data engineers who want pre-built agentic skills in their existing IDE. Teams already on Vertex AI are the immediate audience — the platform is the mandatory upgrade path for all future Vertex AI features. Also relevant for enterprises needing governance controls (agent identity, audit logs, permission management) before deploying agents to business users.
pros: Replaces Vertex AI entirely — all future Google Cloud AI roadmap ships through this platform, so migrating now avoids a forced migration later
Agent Simulation tool lets you stress-test agent behavior against real-world scenarios before shipping to production
Data Agent Kit installs directly in VS Code, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI — no separate infrastructure needed for data engineering agent skills
Built-in A2A (Agent-to-Agent) and MCP protocol support for multi-agent orchestration and private data connectors
cons: Agent Runtime, Memory Bank, Agent Registry, and the optimization suite require enabling the new Agent Platform plan — existing Vertex AI free tiers may not cover these features
Platform is newly GA as of April 22; production-hardening, documentation depth, and community support are still ramping up compared to the mature Vertex AI SDK
detail.setupGuideMarkdown:
## Prerequisites
- Google Cloud account with billing enabled
- `gcloud` CLI installed and authenticated
- `google-cloud-aiplatform` Python SDK (Agent Platform builds on the same SDK)
- Access via [console.cloud.google.com/agent-platform/overview](https://console.cloud.google.com/agent-platform/overview)

```bash
# Install or upgrade the SDK
pip install --upgrade google-cloud-aiplatform google-adk

# Authenticate
gcloud auth application-default login
gcloud config set project YOUR_PROJECT_ID
```

## Install the Data Agent Kit in VS Code

```bash
# Install the Gemini CLI (includes Data Agent Kit plugin support)
npm install -g @google/gemini-cli

# In VS Code, install the "Google Cloud AI" extension from the marketplace
# Then in settings, enable: "Gemini: Data Agent Kit" -> true
# The Data Engineering Agent and Data Science Agent will appear in the sidebar
```

## Build a simple sub-agent network with ADK (Python)

```python
from google.adk.agents import Agent, AgentNetwork
from google.adk.tools import google_search, code_execution

# Define a research sub-agent
research_agent = Agent(
    name="researcher",
    model="gemini-3.1-pro",
    tools=[google_search],
    instruction="Search the web and return structured findings on the given topic.",
)

# Define a synthesis sub-agent
writer_agent = Agent(
    name="writer",
    model="gemini-3.1-pro",
    tools=[code_execution],
    instruction="Take research findings and produce a concise technical summary.",
)

# Wire them into a network
network = AgentNetwork(
    name="research_pipeline",
    agents=[research_agent, writer_agent],
    orchestration="sequential",  # or 'parallel', 'dynamic'
)

result = network.run(input="What are the key differences between TPU v5 and TPU v6?")
print(result.output)
```

## Enable MCP server integration

```python
from google.adk.agents import Agent
from google.adk.tools import MCPServerTool

agent = Agent(
    name="internal_data_agent",
    model="gemini-3.1-pro",
    tools=[
        MCPServerTool(
            server_url="https://your-internal-mcp-server.example.com",
            name="internal_db",
            description="Access to internal CRM and analytics database",
        )
    ],
    instruction="Answer questions using internal company data.",
)

response = agent.run(input="What was last quarter's churn rate by region?")
print(response.output)
```

## Gotchas
- **Vertex AI is not going away immediately**, but all new features ship exclusively through Agent Platform. If you start a new project today, build on Agent Platform from the start.
- **Agent Runtime, Memory Bank, and Registry require the Agent Platform plan** to be enabled in your Google Cloud project. Existing Vertex AI projects see the console rebrand but won't have these features without enabling the plan.
- **MCP server integration is off by default** and requires an Organization Policy Administrator to remove the `constraints/gcp.disableMCPServers` constraint before enabling it.
- **The ADK `google-adk` package is separate from `google-cloud-aiplatform`** — install both if you want local agent testing before deploying to Agent Runtime.

learn.name: OpenClaw
learn.oneLineDescription: Local-first personal AI agent with 50+ integrations that runs entirely on your own hardware.

detail.whatItDoes:
OpenClaw is an open-source personal AI assistant that runs as a local gateway connecting AI models to over 50 integrations including WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, Signal, and iMessage. Your data never leaves your machine. The agent is always on, can browse the web, fill forms, run shell commands, write and execute code, and control desktop apps. Its standout capability is writing its own new skills — it can extend its own functionality without manual intervention.

The project went viral in January 2026, growing from 9,000 to over 60,000 stars in days and has since surpassed 210,000 stars. Originally created by PSPDFKit founder Peter Steinberger, it transitioned to an open-source foundation in February 2026 when Steinberger joined OpenAI.
detail.whoItsFor: Developers and power users who want an always-on local AI agent for workflow automation, web scraping, browser automation, and personal productivity — without sending data to cloud APIs. Also useful for teams in privacy-sensitive environments who need agent capabilities but cannot use hosted services. Requires comfort with self-hosting and reviewing community-contributed skills before enabling them.
pros: Fully local — no data leaves your machine; compatible with local models via Ollama as well as hosted APIs
50+ out-of-the-box integrations covering major messaging platforms and productivity tools
Self-extending: the agent can write and install new skills at runtime, adapting to new tasks without manual code changes
cons: The community skill repository lacks rigorous vetting — security researchers have flagged the risk of malicious skill submissions; review any community skill before enabling it
Broad system permissions required for full functionality (shell access, browser control) — presents a real attack surface on shared or sensitive machines
detail.setupGuideMarkdown:
## Prerequisites
- macOS, Linux, or Windows (Docker recommended for Windows)
- Node.js 20+ and npm
- An API key for at least one AI provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, or a local Ollama instance)

## Install

```bash
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git
cd openclaw

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy and edit config
cp config.example.yml config.yml
```

## Configure your AI provider in config.yml

```yaml
# config.yml
ai:
  provider: openai          # or 'anthropic', 'ollama'
  model: gpt-5.5            # or 'claude-opus-4-6', 'llama3.3' for Ollama
  api_key: "${OPENAI_API_KEY}"

integrations:
  slack:
    enabled: true
    token: "${SLACK_BOT_TOKEN}"
  telegram:
    enabled: false
    token: "${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}"
```

## Set environment variables and start

```bash
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
export SLACK_BOT_TOKEN="xoxb-..."

npm start
# Agent will be available at http://localhost:3000
# Web UI for managing skills and integrations opens automatically
```

## Using Ollama for fully local operation (no cloud API)

```bash
# Install Ollama first: https://ollama.com
ollama pull llama3.3

# In config.yml, set:
# ai:
#   provider: ollama
#   model: llama3.3
#   base_url: http://localhost:11434

npm start
```

## Gotchas
- **Skill vetting is your responsibility.** Before installing any community skill from the skill registry, read its source code. Skills run with the same permissions as the OpenClaw process — a malicious skill has full access to your shell and files.
- **Broad permissions are required by design.** The agent needs browser automation, shell access, and file system access. Do not run OpenClaw on a machine with sensitive credentials stored in plaintext.
- **Apple Silicon Macs** get the best local model performance via Ollama with Metal acceleration. No extra config needed — Ollama auto-detects the GPU.

learn.name: OpenAI Tool Search (Responses API)
learn.oneLineDescription: Defer large tool catalogs to runtime so agents load only what they need, cutting prompt token cost.

detail.whatItDoes:
Tool search is a new Responses API feature (gpt-5.4+ only) that lets agents work with very large tool surfaces without stuffing every tool definition into the context window upfront. You mark tools with `defer_loading: true` inside a namespace or MCP server definition, then add `{"type": "tool_search"}` to your tools array. At inference time, the model emits a `tool_search_call` describing what it needs; your code returns the matching subset of tools; the model then calls them normally.

This is especially useful for enterprise agents that have 50–200+ internal functions, where including all definitions upfront burns tokens, destroys cache hit rates, and degrades reasoning quality. Token savings are most material with namespaces and MCP servers, which is the pattern OpenAI recommends over raw deferred functions.
detail.whoItsFor: Backend engineers building agents with wide tool surfaces — internal CRM connectors, multi-service APIs, large MCP servers. If your current agent prompt is 30%+ tool definitions by token count, tool search will cut costs and improve cache performance immediately. Requires the Responses API (not Chat Completions) and gpt-5.4 or later.
pros: Dramatically reduces prompt token usage on wide-tool-surface agents — only the tools actually needed for the current task are loaded
Preserves prompt cache performance by keeping the static portions of the prompt stable across turns
Works with namespaces, MCP servers, and raw deferred functions — drop-in for existing Responses API integrations
cons: Only available with gpt-5.4 and later — not compatible with older models or the Chat Completions API
Adds a round-trip: the model must emit a tool_search_call, your code resolves the tools, then you send a follow-up request — increases latency by one network hop per tool lookup
detail.setupGuideMarkdown:
## Prerequisites
- `openai` Python SDK >= latest or Node `openai` npm package
- GPT-5.4 or GPT-5.5 (tool_search is not available on older models)
- `OPENAI_API_KEY` set in your environment

```bash
pip install --upgrade openai
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
```

## Pattern 1: Namespace with deferred tools (recommended)

```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

# Define a namespace. Tools with defer_loading=True are hidden
# from the context window until tool_search loads them.
crm_namespace = {
    "type": "namespace",
    "name": "crm",
    "description": "CRM tools for customer lookup and order management.",
    "tools": [
        {
            "type": "function",
            "name": "get_customer_profile",
            "description": "Fetch a customer profile by ID.",
            # No defer_loading — model sees this upfront
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"customer_id": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["customer_id"],
                "additionalProperties": False,
            },
        },
        {
            "type": "function",
            "name": "list_open_orders",
            "description": "List open orders for a customer ID.",
            "defer_loading": True,  # hidden until tool_search loads it
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"customer_id": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["customer_id"],
                "additionalProperties": False,
            },
        },
    ],
}

response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5.5",
    input="List open orders for customer CUST-12345.",
    tools=[
        crm_namespace,
        {"type": "tool_search"},  # enables deferred loading
    ],
)
print(response.output_text)
```

## Pattern 2: MCP server with deferred loading

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5.5",
    input="Pull the Q1 sales data from the warehouse and summarize by region.",
    tools=[
        {
            "type": "mcp",
            "server_label": "data_warehouse",
            "server_url": "https://your-mcp-server.example.com/mcp",
            "defer_loading": True,  # MCP tools loaded via tool_search
            "headers": {"Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_MCP_TOKEN"},
        },
        {"type": "tool_search"},
    ],
)
print(response.output_text)
```

## Gotchas
- **Tool search is only supported on gpt-5.4 and later.** Passing `type: tool_search` to an older model returns a 400 error. Check your model slug before deploying.
- **Adds a round-trip per deferred tool lookup.** For latency-sensitive, real-time user interactions, test whether the token savings outweigh the extra network hop. For batch/async workflows, the savings almost always win.
- **Namespaces and MCP servers get better token savings than raw deferred functions.** The model sees only the namespace/server name and description upfront — not individual function signatures — which gives the largest possible context reduction.
- **Use `parallel_tool_calls=False`** on the first request when using client-side tool_search execution to avoid race conditions during the tool resolution step.
